U Tirot Sing

[Azaadi](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/azaadi/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# U Tirot Sing

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Jul 19, 2021

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

Meghalaya commemorates U Sing Day on July 17, in honor of the Khasi chieftain who led a long resistance against the British in the early 19th century. He was the constitutional head(Syiem) of Nongkhlow, king of the Khadswphra Syiemship, one of the 25 states that made up the Khasi Hills then out of which 15 were presided over by Syiems all elected by popular vote.

His role as Syiem—a title carrying both political and spiritual weight—highlights the distinctive democratic tradition of the Khasi Hills. The fact that 15 of the 25 states elected their Syiems through popular vote speaks volumes about indigenous systems of representation, long before colonial ideas of governance entered the scene.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WCop!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5a63b4ef-c906-4986-8530-b9a799da8fd8_216x233.jpeg)

![Remembering The Immortal Warrior – U Tirot Sing – Vishwa Samvad Kendra,Assam](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WCop!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5a63b4ef-c906-4986-8530-b9a799da8fd8_216x233.jpeg "Remembering The Immortal Warrior – U Tirot Sing – Vishwa Samvad Kendra,Assam")

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!cnO7!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F266b4fa8-4715-45f8-afc2-013eab024e85_636x456.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!cnO7!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F266b4fa8-4715-45f8-afc2-013eab024e85_636x456.png "Image")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Treaty of Yandabo didn’t just mark the end of Anglo-Burmese war in 1926—it redrew boundaries and ambitions. With the British eyeing economic corridors into Assam and the Northeast, David Scott’s appointment as Agent of the Governor General for the entire region between Silchar and Sylhet, and also the Special Civil Comissioner of N.E. Rongpur( Goalpara, Garo Hills)was strategic, almost surgical: consolidating control, facilitating movement, and laying the administrative groundwork for colonial entrenchment.

Scott wanted to build a road connecting Gauhati with Sylhet, at a distance of 230km, that would facilitate travel through the densely forested region. As the road would pass through Meghalaya, he held a meeting with U Tirot Sing.

Nongkhlow is now located in Meghalaya's West Khasi Hills district, around 63km from Shillong, 100 km from Gauhati. The British choose this route, as it would be the fastest to Slyhet as well as the rest of Bengal from Assam.

Tirot Sing was a popular leader among the Khasis, and he had the interets of his people in mind, as he went ahead and accepted the proposal of the British to construct the road passing through his region.

He also felt that the road would be beneficial to the Khasis, giving them better access as well as control over the duars( hilly passes) as well as a viable trade route all the way up to the Surma Valley( now in Bangladesh). By granting passage through Nongkhlow, he opened a corridor toward the Surma Valley, reflecting an astute grasp of geography and diplomacy.

The 1827 durbar with David Scott, joined by fellow chieftains, wasn’t a mere ceremonial gathering—it was a moment of mutual consent rooted in trust and strategic intent. That initial harmony between colonial officers and indigenous leaders feels almost surreal considering what would unfold later.

What began as a promising alliance was slowly undermined by whispers of taxation and outsider labor, eroding the very dignity U Tirot Sing sought to protect. His permission for a road was not a surrender—it was a gesture of goodwill rooted in sovereignty. But the British misread it as submission, overstepping with economic impositions and cultural disregard.

The assault on April 4, 1829, wasn’t merely retaliation—it was a declaration. By striking down Lt. Bedingfield and capturing Lt. Burlton, U Tirot Sing reasserted that Nongkhlow was not for the taking. Scott’s narrow escape from Cherrapunjee underscores how close the revolt came to disrupting colonial designs entirely.

The British immediately bought in troops from Kamrup, Sylhet to put down the revolt. U Tirot Sing and his people knew they were outmatched—archaic weapons against the thunder of British artillery—but they chose defiance over submission, guerrilla warfare over silence. Every ambush in the mist-laced hills became a testament to their will to preserve sovereignty.

The terrain that once promised trade and access now became a battleground of memory, where Khasi warriors etched their resistance into the cliffs and forests. Even as one chief after another laid down arms, the spirit of the revolt lingered, like echoes that refused to fade. The four-year struggle wasn’t just a military episode—it was an existential cry for dignity.

While others surrendered, he chose silence over submission, shadows over shackles. Those caves weren’t just a refuge—they became sanctuaries of resistance, echoing with whispered strategies and unyielding spirit.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CEmK!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7fa45c83-50ec-4a91-a4db-2db84e10575c_1920x1280.jpeg)

![Tirot Sing Cave - Unveiling Nature's Marvel](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CEmK!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7fa45c83-50ec-4a91-a4db-2db84e10575c_1920x1280.jpeg "Tirot Sing Cave - Unveiling Nature's Marvel")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Pmyn!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F480f6ff4-8ed9-49ca-bbb0-82bbe7ae3473_300x168.png "Image")

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!jPZb!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fcbb2d1de-6e53-4fdf-a221-813ecc27904b_680x510.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!jPZb!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fcbb2d1de-6e53-4fdf-a221-813ecc27904b_680x510.png "Image")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Tirot Sing Cave in Nongkhlaw** stands as a living monument to that defiance. Nestled about 62 km from Shillong and 17 km from Mairang, it’s more than a tourist spot—it’s a portal into a forgotten war of courage. Visitors can descend into its depths, navigating narrow passages beneath boulders where the Syiem once evaded colonial pursuit.

The site is best explored during the dry season, and it’s often paired with visits to the **Tirot Sing Memorial in Mairang**, where tributes are paid each year on **July 17**, his death anniversary.

U Tirot Sing hid here for a long time, before he was betrayed by a fellow chieftain, bribed by the British and was forced to surrender on January 9, 1833. was deported to Dhaka, where he ultimately passed away in captivity on July 17, 1835.

The route built by David Scott still exists, and the 16 km stretch between Mawphlang to Lad Mawphlang in the Eastern Khasi Hills, is quite a popular trekking route, passing through hills, valleys, forests.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!YrBx!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc3f338e8-e9b9-4e57-b1fd-705797c2ce36_700x410.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!YrBx!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc3f338e8-e9b9-4e57-b1fd-705797c2ce36_700x410.png "Image")

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!s4dY!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F38999c69-fa69-4a2a-af28-c39819231bb9_800x400.png "Image")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Today, **Tirot Sing Day** is solemnly observed across Meghalaya every **July 17**, honoring his courage and sacrifice. The state not only commemorates him with ceremonies and cultural tributes, but also upholds his legacy through the **U Tirot Sing Award for Arts and Literature**, celebrating creators who embody the spirit of resistance, identity, and cultural pride.

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)
